quizzes.title: 5.2 Listing Outcomes and Sample Space Diagrams

questions.prompt: How many possible outcomes are there when flipping a coin and rolling a six-sided dice together?
A sample space diagram is used to show:
options: The probability of a single event
All possible outcomes of two combined events
The mean of a data set

questions.prompt: How many outcomes are there when rolling two six-sided dice?
Listing all possible outcomes when choosing 1 item from {red, blue, green} and 1 item from {small, large} gives how many combinations?
A sample space diagram can help calculate the probability of combined events.
When flipping two coins, there are 3 possible outcomes.
Listing outcomes systematically helps avoid missing or repeating possibilities.
List all possible outcomes when flipping a coin twice.
How many outcomes are possible when choosing a starter (3 options) and a main course (4 options)?
Two dice are rolled and their scores added. How many possible totals range from 2 to 12 inclusive?

quizzes.title: 5.3 The AND/OR Rules and Venn Diagrams

questions.prompt: For mutually exclusive events A and B, P(A or B) =
For independent events A and B, P(A and B) =
In a Venn diagram, the intersection of two sets represents:
options: All elements in the universal set

questions.prompt: The probability of rolling a 2 or a 5 on a fair six-sided dice is:
Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.
Independent events affect each other's probability of occurring.
The probabilities of all mutually exclusive outcomes in a sample space sum to 1.
A bag has 5 red, 3 blue, and 2 green balls (10 total). Find the probability of picking red or blue.
Two independent events have probabilities 0.4 and 0.5. Find the probability that both happen.
In a class of 30, 12 study French, 10 study Spanish, and 5 study both. How many study French or Spanish (or both)?
Relative frequency is calculated as:
options: total number of trials ÷ number of successful trials
number of successful trials ÷ total number of trials
number of successful trials × total trials

questions.prompt: A dice is rolled 50 times and lands on 6 a total of 12 times. What is the relative frequency of rolling a 6?
As the number of trials increases, relative frequency generally:
options: Stays exactly the same every time
Gets closer to the true theoretical probability

questions.prompt: Experimental probability is also known as:
Relative frequency can be used to estimate probability when a fair outcome cannot be assumed (e.g. a biased dice).
Theoretical probability is always calculated by carrying out an experiment.
A larger number of trials generally gives a more reliable estimate of probability.
A coin is flipped 40 times and lands on heads 18 times. Find the relative frequency of heads.
A spinner is spun 200 times and lands on red 50 times. Estimate the probability of landing on red.
Using a relative frequency of 0.3 from 20 trials, estimate the expected number of successes in 500 trials.
Tree diagrams are used to show:
On a tree diagram, to find the probability of a particular path (branch), you:
options: Add the probabilities along the branches
Multiply the probabilities along the branches

questions.prompt: A bag has a 0.3 probability of red and 0.7 of blue. Two balls are picked with replacement. What is the probability of picking red then blue?
On a probability tree diagram, all branches from a single point must sum to:
'With replacement' means the probabilities stay the same for each pick.
'Without replacement' means the probabilities can change between successive picks.
To find the probability of two different paths on a tree diagram, you multiply their individual probabilities together.
A spinner has P(win)=0.4. It is spun twice. Find the probability of winning both times.
A bag has 4 red and 6 blue balls (10 total). Two are picked without replacement. Find the probability both are red.
A coin is flipped twice. Find the probability of getting exactly one head (in either order).

quizzes.title: 6.1 Collecting Data and Types of Data

questions.prompt: Data that can be counted in whole numbers (e.g. number of pets) is called:
Data that can take any value within a range (e.g. height, weight) is called:
A questionnaire question like 'Do you like sport? Yes/No' collects:
Which of these is an example of primary data?
options: Data you collect yourself through a survey
Data taken from a published report
Data found on a government website

questions.prompt: A sample should be representative of the population it is taken from.
Continuous data can only take specific, separate values, like shoe sizes.
Secondary data is data that has already been collected by someone else.
Give an example of discrete data.
Give an example of continuous data.
What term describes a small part of a population selected for a survey?
A frequency table shows:
options: Only the mean of the data
How often each value or category occurs

questions.prompt: In a bar chart, the height of each bar represents:
What is a key difference between a bar chart and a histogram?
options: Bar charts are only for continuous data
Bar charts have gaps between bars; histograms (for continuous data) do not

questions.prompt: A two-way table is used to show:
options: The relationship between two categorical variables

questions.prompt: In a bar chart, bars are typically drawn with equal width and spaced with gaps between them.
A tally chart is a quick way to record data as it is being collected.
A composite (stacked) bar chart can only show one category at a time.
In a survey, 15 people chose 'tea', 10 chose 'coffee', and 5 chose 'juice'. What is the total number of people surveyed?
What type of chart uses tallies grouped in fives to record frequency?
A two-way table shows data about gender and favourite subject. What is this type of table used to compare?
A full circle in a pie chart represents:
To find the angle for a category in a pie chart, you calculate:
A survey of 60 people shows 15 prefer cats. What angle represents this on a pie chart?
Pie charts are most useful for showing:
All the angles in a pie chart must add up to 360°.
A pie chart can show exact frequency counts directly without any calculation.
A larger sector in a pie chart represents a larger proportion of the data.
A pie chart shows a sector with an angle of 72°. What fraction of the total does this represent?
In a survey of 40 people, 8 chose 'walking' as their favourite exercise. What angle represents this on a pie chart?
A pie chart sector has an angle of 180°. If the total sample size is 50, how many people does this sector represent?
The mean of a data set is calculated as:
options: sum of all values ÷ number of values
the highest value minus the lowest

questions.prompt: The median is:
options: The middle value when data is ordered

questions.prompt: The mode is:
Find the range of this data set: 4, 8, 2, 10, 6.
A data set can have more than one mode.
The range is calculated as the highest value plus the lowest value.
The median is not affected by extreme outlier values as much as the mean is.
Find the mean of: 3, 7, 5, 9, 6
Find the median of: 2, 9, 4, 7, 5
Find the mode of: 3, 5, 5, 7, 8, 5, 2
To estimate the mean from a grouped frequency table, you use:
options: The midpoint of each class multiplied by frequency
The highest value in each class

questions.prompt: In a frequency table, the modal class is:
options: The class with the lowest frequency
The class with the highest frequency

questions.prompt: A frequency table shows 5 people scored in the range 0-10, and 12 scored in the range 40-50. Estimate the total number of people, if these are the only two classes.
Why is the mean from a grouped frequency table called an 'estimate'?
options: Because exact individual data values are unknown, only the class they fall in
Because grouped data is always inaccurate
Because frequency tables cannot be used for averages
Because the mean is always a whole number

questions.prompt: The midpoint of a class is found by adding the upper and lower boundaries and dividing by 2.
You can find an exact mean (not an estimate) from grouped, continuous data.
The modal class in a grouped frequency table is the class with the highest frequency.
A class interval is 10-20. What is its midpoint?
A frequency table has class '20-30' with frequency 8, and midpoint 25. What is the value of frequency × midpoint for this class?
What term describes the total of (frequency × midpoint) divided by the total frequency, used to estimate an average?
A scatter graph is used to show:
options: The frequency of a single variable
The relationship between two sets of data

questions.prompt: If points on a scatter graph generally rise from left to right, this shows:
If points on a scatter graph generally fall from left to right, this shows:
A line of best fit is used to:
options: Show the general trend and help make predictions
Show the mode of the data
Replace the need for a scatter graph

questions.prompt: Correlation shows that one variable directly causes changes in the other.
A scatter graph with points randomly spread with no pattern shows no correlation.
A line of best fit should have roughly equal numbers of points above and below it.
A scatter graph shows that as temperature increases, ice cream sales increase. What type of correlation is this?
A scatter graph shows that as speed increases, journey time decreases. What type of correlation is this?
What is the term for a data point on a scatter graph that doesn't fit the general trend?
A time series graph always has which variable on the horizontal axis?
Time series graphs are useful for showing:
options: Only a single moment in time

questions.prompt: A repeating pattern in data that occurs at regular time intervals (e.g. every summer) is called:
A moving average is used on a time series graph to:
options: Smooth out short-term fluctuations and show the underlying trend
Replace the need for a graph

questions.prompt: Time series graphs can be used to make predictions about future trends.
Points on a time series graph are usually joined in the order they occur over time.
Seasonality in a time series refers to random, unpredictable changes in data.
What is plotted on the vertical axis of a time series graph showing monthly sales?
What term describes the general long-term direction shown by a time series graph (e.g. upward or downward)?
Give one example of a seasonal pattern you might see in a time series of ice cream sales over a year.
acceptedAnswers: sales peak in summer, lower in winter
